title: Senegal bans ministers from foreign travel as oil price rise bites

originalTitle: 'We want a voice in our land' - the people evicted to build Nigeria's capital
title: Nigeria's Capital City: A Big Story About Homes!
content:
Hello from Africa!

Hi everyone! It's your friendly news reporter from amazing Africa! Today, we have an important story from a big country called Nigeria. Nigeria is a country in West Africa, full of exciting people and places. It's a land of sunshine and smiles, but sometimes, there are big stories that need our attention. This story is about homes, promises, and making sure everyone is happy and heard. *Did you know?* Nigeria is often called the "Giant of Africa" because it has so many people!

What is a Capital City?

Every country needs a very special city called a "capital city." This is like the brain of the country! It's where the president lives and works. It's where important decisions are made for everyone in the country. Imagine a big office for the whole country – that's often what a capital city feels like. For a long time, the capital city of Nigeria was a place called Lagos. Lagos is a very, very busy city! It's next to the ocean and full of people and cars. *Did you know?* Lagos is one of the biggest cities in all of Africa!

Meet the People Who Lived There First

A long, long time ago, where Nigeria's new capital city is today, there were many small villages. Many families lived there. They built their homes, grew their food, and played with their friends. They had schools and markets. Their families had lived there for many, many years, perhaps even for hundreds of years! They loved their land. It was their home, where their grandmas and grandpas had lived too. It was a peaceful place, full of life and laughter.

Building a Brand New City: Abuja!

One day, the government of Nigeria decided to build a *brand new* capital city. They wanted a city that was central for everyone, easy to get to from all parts of the country. They chose a beautiful spot in the middle of Nigeria. This new city would be called Abuja. It was going to be very grand, with tall buildings, wide roads, and big parks. It would be a city everyone in Nigeria could be proud of. But for the new city of Abuja to be built, the people who lived in those villages had to move. They had to leave their homes. This is what we call being "evicted." It means being asked or told to leave your home, even if you don't want to. It was a very big change for them.

What is a Promise?

A promise is a very important thing. When someone makes a promise, it means they say they will do something. And when you make a promise, you really, really should keep it! It makes people trust you. If a friend promises to share their toys, you expect them to do it, right? It's the same for big people and governments. Promises help everyone know what to expect. They help us feel safe and fair.

The Promises That Were Made

When the government told the people they had to leave their homes for Abuja, they also made some big promises. They said, "Don't worry! We will help you. We will give you new homes, or new land, or money to build new lives." They promised to make sure these families would be okay. These promises were very important because it meant the people had to trust the government with their future. They packed up their belongings, said goodbye to their old homes, and moved, believing in these promises. It was hard to leave, but they hoped for a good new start.

Feeling Sad and Unheard

Now, many, many years have passed since the big city of Abuja was built. Abuja is a beautiful city, bustling with life. But sadly, many of the people who were evicted say that the government did not keep all of its promises. They are very sad and feel like they were forgotten. Some people did not get new homes. Some did not get new land. Some did not get the money they were promised. They feel like their voices are not being heard. Imagine if you were promised a new toy, but it never came. You would feel disappointed, wouldn't you? These people feel a much bigger kind of disappointment because it's about their homes and their future. *Did you know?* Abuja is famous for a big rock called Aso Rock, which you can see from many parts of the city!

What Do the People Want Now?

These brave people want the government to remember them. They want the government to keep its promises, even now, many years later. They want to be heard. They are asking for their land back, or for new places to live that are good and safe. They want help to build new lives, just like they were promised a long time ago. They want to have a "voice in their land" – meaning they want to be listened to and have a say in what happens to them. They believe it is fair for the government to help them now, especially since they helped the country build its beautiful capital.

Why This Story is Important for Everyone

This story is important for all of us, even if we live far away. It teaches us about fairness. Everyone deserves a safe home. Everyone deserves to be treated with kindness. And promises are very important – especially big promises made by people in charge. When promises are broken, it can make people very sad and feel like they don't matter. It reminds us that we should always try to keep our promises and listen to people who are sad or need help. It's about making sure that as countries grow and change, no one is left behind.

Let's Hope for a Happy Ending!

We hope that the government of Nigeria will listen to these people. We hope they will remember their promises and help these families. Every person's home and happiness matter. Let's send our good wishes to the people of Nigeria, hoping for a fair and happy ending to this important story. It's a reminder that we should always speak up for what is right!
summary: People who had to leave their homes to build Nigeria's big capital city, Abuja, are sad because they say the government did not keep its promises to help them.
whyMatters: This story matters because everyone deserves a safe home and for promises to be kept, especially by leaders, so that all people are treated fairly and heard.
